category_title: Children who speak unintelligibly due to severe phonological impairments
scenario_title: Spontaneous toy-based conversation in therapy room
minimal_context: The student and tutor are in the therapy room having a spontaneous conversation about the toys present.

dialogue.speaker: tutor
dialogue.message: Here are the toys we can play with today. What should we do with the blocks and cars?

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: Dah wuh bee gah. (Speaks a 4-word unintelligible utterance confidently, without using any gestures)

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: Dah wuh bee gah. (Repeats the exact same verbal message, believing the words are coming out just as intended)

dialogue.speaker: tutor
dialogue.message: I still don't understand what you mean.

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: Dah wuh bee gah! (Repeats the verbal message again, but this time emphasizes the content words by pointing directly at the car and showing where it should go)

dialogue_combined:
TUTOR: Here are the toys we can play with today. What should we do with the blocks and cars?

STUDENT: Dah wuh bee gah. (Speaks a 4-word unintelligible utterance confidently, without using any gestures)

TUTOR: What did you say?

STUDENT: Dah wuh bee gah. (Repeats the exact same verbal message, believing the words are coming out just as intended)

TUTOR: I still don't understand what you mean.

STUDENT: Dah wuh bee gah! (Repeats the verbal message again, but this time emphasizes the content words by pointing directly at the car and showing where it should go)

task.content: The subject of conversation was not controlled. In most cases, the toys present in the therapy room served as topics of conversation. Sometimes a conversation emerged spontaneously, independent of the toys.

context.materials: toys present in the therapy room
context.social_setting: Two speech therapists (one being one of the researchers/authors) worked with four children each.

challenging_behavior_trigger.condition: when their conversational partner misunderstood them
challenging_behavior_trigger.observable_behavior: repeating their verbal message, they emphasized content words by supporting them with deictic and iconic gestures... added deictic gestures to their speech – like pointing and showing – to support key words
challenging_behavior_trigger.internal_state: unaware of their speech problems. These children believe that the message ‘in their heads’ actually comes out just as they had intended.
related_quotes: believe that the message ‘in their heads’ actually comes out just as they had intended

behavior_patterns.condition: During baseline communication before explicit intervention
behavior_patterns.observable_behavior: used more than 40 verbal utterances out of the total 50 utterances... Their speech consisted of 3- to 5-word utterances. However, these children hardly used the ‘gesture/sign’ mode to communicate.
only looked for an alternative when they were misunderstood and needed a complementary mode of communication to support their verbal message.
